region_of_origin: Thailand — a classic Thai tea house and street food beverage, widely available at Singapore Thai restaurants and Thai tea cafés
food_subgroup: Sweetened milk tea — strongly brewed Thai tea mix with condensed milk and evaporated milk, served over ice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Cha Yen. Closest parent is a sweetened milk tea beverage. No URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tea-based beverage — strongly brewed Thai tea (Ceylon tea with star anise and tamarind) sweetened with condensed and evaporated milk, served over ice. FoodEx2 A0GRC (tea and tea-based beverages) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Cha Yen. A0GRC (tea-based beverages) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Cha Yen or Thai iced milk tea. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

main_ingredients: Thai tea mix (Ceylon black tea with star anise, tamarind, orange blossom, food colouring)

serving.description: 1 glass = 400 g liquid (500 ml glass with ice displacement)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is one tall glass (approximately 400 g of liquid after ice displacement from a 500 ml glass).

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia — Tea, milk, sweetened (Thai iced milk tea, Cha Yen)
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced from FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table for sweetened Thai-style milk tea (Cha Yen). The entry covers strongly brewed Ceylon-based Thai tea mix combined with sweetened condensed milk and evaporated milk in proportions consistent with standard Thai tea house preparation. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not reported in this FAO/INFOODS entry; these fields are coded -1 to indicate not reported by source.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — Sweetened condensed milk tea (analogous estimate: high-sugar sweetened milk beverage, GI ~88)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Cha Yen in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 88 is estimated from the closest analogous high-sugar sweetened milk beverage entries in the University of Sydney GI Database, reflecting the dominant glycaemic driver of sucrose from sweetened condensed milk (glucose + fructose from sucrose hydrolysis). Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (88 × 60.0) / 100 = 52.8, where carbohydrate per serving = 15.0 g/100g × 400 g serving = 60.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 52 mg/100 g; 208 mg per 400 g serving
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content is low at 52 mg/100 g, delivering only 208 mg per standard 400 g serving — well below the 600 mg per-meal threshold of concern; calcium from condensed and evaporated milk (78 mg/100 g; 312 mg per serving) may provide a modest blood-pressure benefit, making Cha Yen a low hypertension concern when consumed occasionally.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI 88 (high), glycaemic load 52.8 per serving, 60 g available carbohydrate per serving with zero dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cha Yen carries a very high glycaemic load of 52.8 per standard 400 g serving — equivalent to the glycaemic impact of approximately 15 teaspoons of sugar dissolved in water — driven by 60 g of rapidly absorbed carbohydrate (predominantly sucrose from sweetened condensed milk) with no dietary fibre to attenuate the glucose response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit consumption and consider unsweetened alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.0 g/100 g (8.0 g per serving); high added sugar 14.2 g/100 g (56.8 g per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A single 400 g serving of Cha Yen delivers approximately 8.0 g of saturated fat and 56.8 g of sugar — both of which, in regular consumption, are associated with elevated LDL cholesterol and triglyceride levels; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should treat Cha Yen as an occasional indulgence rather than a daily beverage.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cha Yen (ชาเย็น), known in English as Thai Iced Milk Tea, is one of Thailand's most iconic and visually distinctive beverages — a rich, sweet, creamy, intensely orange-coloured chilled tea that has become a cultural symbol of Thai food culture and is widely served at Thai restaurants and Thai tea cafés across Singapore. The tea base is a strongly brewed Thai tea mix, a proprietary blend of Ceylon black tea combined with small quantities of star anise, tamarind seed, orange blossom water, and a yellow-orange food colouring (typically annatto-based or synthetic) that produces the characteristic vivid orange hue. The brewed tea is poured over a generous measure of sweetened condensed milk and evaporated milk in a tall glass packed with crushed ice, creating dramatic swirling ribbons of cream before being stirred into a smooth, golden-orange drink. Nutritionally, Cha Yen is one of the highest-sugar café beverages available: a standard 400 g serving contains approximately 60 g of carbohydrate (predominantly sucrose from condensed milk), 8 g of saturated fat, and delivers a glycaemic load of 52.8 — comparable to consuming 15 teaspoons of sugar in liquid form. The GI is estimated at approximately 88 (high), making it a significant concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. Sodium is low (208 mg per serving), and the dairy content provides useful calcium (312 mg per serving). In Singapore, Cha Yen is available at Thai restaurants, dedicated Thai tea shops (particularly around Orchard Road and Golden Mile Complex), and bubble tea outlets offering Thai tea flavours. The food colouring responsible for the orange colour has no nutritional significance.

sweetened condensed milk: นมข้นหวาน (nom khen wan)
evaporated milk: นมข้นจืด (nom khen juet)

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are sourced from the FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table for sweetened Thai-style milk tea, which is the highest-priority approved regional source for this dish. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (no direct Cha Yen entry exists); confidence is therefore medium rather than high. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not reported in the source entry.
notes: Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are coded -1 because these values are not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source entry for this food. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g (confirmed negligible in dairy-based milk tea at typical preparation ratios). GI of 88 is an analogous estimate; a direct peer-reviewed GI measurement for Cha Yen has not been identified in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date. The food colouring in Thai tea mix (annatto or synthetic orange dye) has no nutritional impact and is not reflected in nutrient values.
